elements.sent-2411-001: Distributed systems often serve dynamic workloads and resource demands evolve over time.
elements.sent-2411-002: Such a temporal behavior stands in contrast to the static and demand-oblivious nature of most data structures used by these systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we are particularly interested in consistent hashing, a fundamental building block in many large distributed systems.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our work is motivated by the hypothesis that a more adaptive approach to consistent hashing can leverage structure in the demand, and hence improve storage utilization and reduce access time.
elements.sent-2411-005: We initiate the study of demand-aware consistent hashing.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our main contribution is H\&A, a~constant-competitive online algorithm (i.e., it comes with provable performance guarantees over time).
elements.sent-2411-007: H\&A is demand-aware and optimizes its internal structure to enable faster access times, while offering a high utilization of storage.
elements.sent-2411-009: Access patterns and demand in distributed systems are often dynamic and feature structure over time~\cite{denning1968working,Locality99, nicopolitidis2006exploiting,Locality12,roy2015inside,sigmetrics20complexity,Locality22,SeedTree,alenex25,OPODIS25 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Such dynamicity and temporal locality of demand have been observed in many application domains and encouraged the design of optimized computer architectures that support such structure, e.g., through a cache hierarchy.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our paper is motivated by the hypothesis that there is still much-untapped potential for adaptive and demand-aware approaches for optimizing the performance of data-centric applications~\cite{avin2018toward .}
elements.sent-2411-012: We are particularly interested in consistent hashing~\cite{KargerClassic1 , a key component of many distributed systems, which has applications in systems such as Amazon DynamoDB~\cite{Dynamo07, DynamoDB12 and Apache Cassandra~\cite{Cassandra10 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Consistent hashing aims to provide fast lookups on top of flexible insertion and deletions, making it especially attractive for web applications and distributed databases~\cite{WebCaching99 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, consistent hashing today is still mostly demand-oblivious and has shortcomings, e.g., related to the high variance in the load of different servers, which can result in sub-optimal utilization.
elements.sent-2411-015: This paper envisions a more demand-aware approach to consistent hashing, facilitating the adaptation to---and exploitation of---structure in the demand, with the goal of reducing access costs and improving load balancing (i.e., maximizing storage utilization).
elements.sent-2411-016: Our work is further motivated by a recent work by Mirrokni, Thorup and Zadimoghaddam~\cite{MirrokniTZ18 that showed that bounding server capacities within a factor of the minimum needed capacity (i.e., the number of items divided by the number of servers) is possible given a tolerable access cost increase.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Considering access costs is a new dimension in the design of consistent hashing methods that we further develop in this work.
elements.sent-2411-018: We show that our demand-aware approach can improve storage utilization while keeping the access cost competitive.
elements.sent-2411-019: Designing a demand-aware variant of consistent hashing is, however, challenging.
elements.sent-2411-020: This is because the demand is not perfectly known ahead of time.
elements.sent-2411-021: Therefore such a design requires a careful balancing of the benefits of adjustment and the cost of adjustments~\cite{Roughgarden19 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our main contribution is Hash \& Adjust (\system for short), an online and demand-aware algorithm for consistent hashing.
elements.sent-2411-023: The performance of \system differs only by a constant factor from the optimal offline algorithm (that knows the whole demand in advance), i.e., \system is constant competitive.
elements.sent-2411-024: Constant competitiveness becomes possible in our system by keeping the recently accessed items close to their original server via self adjustments, in the spirit of strategies known from list update problems~\cite{SleatorT85,Albers03OnlineBook .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Local adjustments are crucial for its practical use cases in distributed systems, due to their storage and computation efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-027: To achieve this, we extend list access in a novel direction: list access with multiple heads and with capacity.
elements.sent-2411-028: This extension can be of independent interest in other application scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-029: We complement our theoretical contribution with an empirical comparison between the performance of \system with state-of-the-art algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our empirical evaluations indicate an average $54\%$ improvement in the access cost (i.e., item access time) compared to the recent consistent hashing method~\cite{MirrokniTZ18 (see details in the ~\Cref{sec: implement ).}}
elements.sent-2411-031: This section motivates the need to critically reconsider today's consistent hashing methods.
elements.sent-2411-032: We begin with a short description of the current state of consistent hashing methods, detailed in more depth in Section~\ref{sec: related .. Basic consistent hashing.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In the basic version of consistent hashing~\cite{KargerClassic1 , servers (e.g., nodes of a system) are arranged over a ring, hosting a set of items (e.g., DNS records).}
elements.sent-2411-034: Operations on an item begin by computing the hash value for the item.
elements.sent-2411-035: Then, based on the hash value, the item is assigned to the closest server (in a clockwise manner, details follow in the next section).
elements.sent-2411-036: In practice, the requests are generated in a distributed manner: any server can initiate the request, run the hash function, and find the target server using the network that is built on top of the ring.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this work, following the lead of recent advancements~\cite{MirrokniTZ18, AamandKT21 , we focus on ring abstraction.}
elements.sent-2411-038: With this abstraction, we provide flexibility of choice: we can connect or even better: nodes on the ring can be connected to form a desired network of choice.
elements.sent-2411-039: Hence, we remove the dependence on a particular network and analyze a consistent hashing instance in its most general form.
elements.sent-2411-040: Consistent hashing operations are history independent and local.
elements.sent-2411-041: This is because a single shared hash function suffices to determine the server that hosts an item.
elements.sent-2411-042: Furthermore, consistent hashing is preferable to other distributed hashing methods as it allows for online insertion and deletions.
elements.sent-2411-043: However, traditional consistent hashing methods suffer from low storage utilization.
elements.sent-2411-044: Therefore, we also evaluate server loads (i.e., the number of items in a server).. Suboptimal storage utilization.
elements.sent-2411-045: A high variance in server loads implies a suboptimal storage utilization and is generally undesirable in practice.
elements.sent-2411-046: To this end, we define server capacity as the maximum number of items a server can hold.
elements.sent-2411-047: Since the server with the maximum load can not be predetermined, we need to reserve the capacity equal to the maximum load for all servers.
elements.sent-2411-048: As the sum of the load of all servers is equal to the number of items, we define the storage utilization as: \begin{equation}\label{eg: load} \frac{\text{Total Load}}{\text{Total Capacity}} = \frac{\text{Number of Items}}{\text{Maximum Load * Number of Servers}} = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Maximum Load}}\end{equation} For a fair comparison, we consider an equal number of items and servers for all algorithms.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eg: load} \frac{Total Load}{Total Capacity} = \frac{Number of Items}{Maximum Load * Number of Servers} = \frac{Average Load}{Maximum Load}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-049: Hence, given Equation~\ref{eg: load , we focus on the maximum load of algorithms.}

elements.sent-2411-050: It is well known that traditional consistent hashing has a non-constant difference between the average and maximum load~\cite{harvey2022book .}
elements.sent-2411-051: This is why a recent work by Mirrokni~et~al.~\cite{MirrokniTZ18 suggested bounding the load of each server by a constant factor times the minimum load needed (we call their work With Bounded Loads, or ``WBL" for short).}
elements.sent-2411-052: However, the difference remains limited between \WBL's proposal and the case with unbounded capacity We emphasize that bounding loads aim to adhere to stringent storage constraints of reserved storage resources, that can not be achieved through flexible storage allocation methods~\cite{on-demand1 .. Increased access time.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The idea of bounding server capacity might seem obvious at first sight.
elements.sent-2411-054: However, a challenge arises: as servers become full, we need to put the items of the oversubscribed server elsewhere, otherwise data may be lost.
elements.sent-2411-055: To model this issue more formally, we define access time as the number of servers that one needs to traverse to find an item.
elements.sent-2411-056: This is a fair consideration, as moving items between servers requires noticeably more time than searching within a server for an item.
elements.sent-2411-057: Also, moving items between servers should be local: without a centralized map of items or separate server assignment functions.
elements.sent-2411-058: Fortunately, however, real-world request sequences have an inherent temporal locality.
elements.sent-2411-059: This means that among items that are assigned to a server, we have intervals of consecutive requests for the same item -- see~\cite{sigmetrics20complexity for a study of the temporal locality of real-world instances.. Overview of \system.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The consistent hashing method proposed in this work relies on an online algorithm, \system.
elements.sent-2411-061: Our method improves storage utilization by considering only an additive additional capacity (e.g., considering only $2$ more storage slots per server) rather than the multiplicative additional capacity proposed previously.
elements.sent-2411-062: Thus, we can push the storage utilization to its limit.
elements.sent-2411-063: To reduce access time, \system self-adjusts items between servers after every request -- not only upon insertion or deletion.
elements.sent-2411-064: This way, we ensure a decreased overall average cost, especially in the case of a high temporal locality.
elements.sent-2411-065: In this section, we present our theoretical model and introduce the required terminologies and preliminaries.. Consistent hashing.
elements.sent-2411-066: We consider a set of $m$ items $V$, and a set $S$ of $n$ servers.
elements.sent-2411-067: As in practice, the number of items is much larger than the number of servers; we consider $m$ to be significantly greater than $n$, i.e., $m \gg n$.
elements.sent-2411-068: We assign a head to each item.
elements.sent-2411-069: The head of an item is a server that has the closest hash value to the hash value of the item.
elements.sent-2411-070: In our analysis, we consider a hash function $\myHash$, that hashes the ID of a server or an item to a value in the range $[0,1]$ uniformly at random\footnote{This is a common~\cite{MirrokniTZ18 assumption, given that there are hash functions that can provide almost uniformly at random results~\cite{5Independent1 .. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Formally, the head $s$ for an item $v$ is selected as follows: \begin{equation}\head{v} = \argmin_{s \in S} \ \myHash{s}-\myHash{v} \mod 1\end{equation} In other words, the function $\mymyHash$ maps items and servers on a ring, and the head of an item is a server on the ring closest to the item in a clockwise manner.
elements.sent-2411-072: We emphasize that this process is carried out in a distributed fashion, i.e., the hash function is known globally, not solely to a~central entity.
elements.sent-2411-073: Furthermore, we define the server that hosts item $v$ at time $t$ as $\hostt{v}$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Considering a fixed $\mymyHash$ function, servers maintain a~certain order between them.
elements.sent-2411-075: Given a server $s$, we denote the server after server $s$ by $s^+$ and the server before $s$ by $s^-$.. Bounding servers' capacity.
elements.sent-2411-076: All servers can contain up to $c = \lceil \frac{m {n \rceil + \alpha$ items, i.e., they have capacity equal to $c$.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Variable $\alpha \ge 1$ is the additive extra capacity which is fixed throughout the run time of \system.
elements.sent-2411-078: See Figure~\ref{fig: model for an example.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Considering additive extra capacity results in a tradeoff between storage utilization and access cost.
elements.sent-2411-080: Increasing the value of $\alpha$ would lower the access cost of the system (as items will be stored closer to their head), but requires more storage per server.. Online requests sequence.
elements.sent-2411-081: We consider a significantly large request sequence $\sigma = (\sigma_1,\dots)$, in which $\sigma_t$ is the request at time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-082: Requests can either refer to an item or a server, and they might have one of three types:.
elements.sent-2411-083: Access to an item: searching for an item inside the system,.
elements.sent-2411-084: Inserting a server or an item: adding a new server or an item that was not in the system before,.
elements.sent-2411-085: Deleting a server or an item: removing one of the existing items or servers from the system.
elements.sent-2411-086: It is important to respond to requests locally and immediately, i.e., not requiring a global view or a (estimation of) future knowledge when updating the data structure, as they are critical for distributed settings.
elements.sent-2411-087: Furthermore, we assume that insertion and deletion operations are happening rarely~\cite{RevisitCH21 , in particular after every $\sum_{i=1 ^{n-1 e^{-\frac{\alpha^2 {2m (i+1)}$ access operation.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: While responding to requests, a system might reconfigure some items, i.e., moving them from one server to another.. Cost model.
elements.sent-2411-089: Responding to a request comes at a delay: we might need to search a few servers to find the item or move items around after insertion and deletion requests.
elements.sent-2411-090: Therefore, we consider two types of delay:.
elements.sent-2411-091: Search delay: the delay that occurs while searching and retrieving data from a server.
elements.sent-2411-092: In our abstraction, we consider the access delay as the number of servers traversed to find an item, including the head of an item.
elements.sent-2411-093: Reconfiguration delay: the delay of moving items between servers.
elements.sent-2411-094: Similar to the access delay, we only consider the number of servers that we move an item between.
elements.sent-2411-095: We emphasize that all the delays in any operation (access, insertion, and deletion) are essentially either a search delay or a reconfiguration delay.
elements.sent-2411-096: We abstract the delay we observe for a request as the cost of that request.
elements.sent-2411-097: The cost of searching or moving items inside a server is negligible compared to the inter-server costs.
elements.sent-2411-098: We point out, that in our model, accessing the head of an item has a cost of $1$, for any algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-099: Given that transferring data costs more than accessing it, we consider a constant factor $\omega > 1$ for the cost of relocating a single item from a server to a neighboring server compared to the cost of going from one server to another searching for an item (this is another extension of the previous consistent hashing models~\cite{MirrokniTZ18, AamandKT21 , which only considered $\omega=1$).}
elements.sent-2411-100: Hence, the total cost of an algorithm $ALG$ for all its operations over a request sequence is: \begin{equation}\label{eq: total cost} C_{ALG}(\sigma) = C^{\text{Search}}_{ALG}(\sigma) + \omega \cdot C^{\text{Reconfiguration}}_{ALG}(\sigma)\end{equation} Our aim is to minimize the total cost of our online algorithm compared to the optimal offline algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-101: We consider that the inputs are generated by an oblivious adversary.
elements.sent-2411-102: The oblivious adversary is not aware of the random bits used by an algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-103: This is a common assumption, as there are opportunities for pseudorandom number generation (for details on different types of adversaries, check~\cite{borodin2005onlineBook ).}
elements.sent-2411-104: Formally, our objective is to develop an algorithm with a constant competitive ratio, i.e., we want the cost of our algorithm to match the optimal offline algorithm asymptotically.
elements.sent-2411-105: Denote the total cost of $ALG$ over an input sequence $\sigma$ as $C_{ALG (\sigma)$, and similarly the cost of $OPT$ as $C_{OPT (\sigma)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Then the (strict) competitive ratio of $ALG$ is defined as: $\max_{\sigma \frac{C_{ALG (\sigma) {C_{OPT (\sigma)}$.. \end{mydefinition.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: In this section, we first discuss how access operations are done in \system, and then discuss insertion and deletion operations.
elements.sent-2411-108: We start by describing how we handle an access request in \system.
elements.sent-2411-109: The main innovation of our approach is to use self-adjustments after each access request.
elements.sent-2411-111: With self-adjustments, we aim to reduce the access cost by adjusting the position of items inside the data structure.
elements.sent-2411-112: In particular, we look at a self-adjustment procedure that brings back items to their heads step by step, from a server to its neighbor on the ring.
elements.sent-2411-113: We consider self-adjustments on a ring as an abstraction (rather than considering a fixed network used in distributed hash tables).
elements.sent-2411-114: This is a plus: it provides a flexible choice for the network that can be built on top, as each application in peer-to-peer usecases uses a different overlay network (Chord, Kademlia, etc.).. Accessing an item.
elements.sent-2411-115: After our algorithm (Algorithm~\ref{Alg: MTHC ) receives access to an item $v$, it starts looking for $v$ inside the head assigned to it, $s = \head{v}$.}
elements.sent-2411-116: From then on, for any given server $s$, \system checks for the following three possibilities in order:.
elements.sent-2411-117: Item $v$ is not in server $s$ and $s$ is full.
elements.sent-2411-118: Then, item $v$ might have been moved to server $s^+$, so we set $s = s^+$ and start checking again.
elements.sent-2411-119: Item $v$ is not in server $s$ and $s$ is not full.
elements.sent-2411-120: This means that item $v$ is not in our system.
elements.sent-2411-122: Then we successfully found the item.
elements.sent-2411-123: If $s \neq \head{v}$, then we swap $v$ with the least recently accessed item in server $s^-$, $u$, through a $swap(u,v)$ operation.
elements.sent-2411-124: The swap operation for items $u$ and $v$ works for two items with adjacent servers.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{\system access to an item $v$} \label{Alg: MTHC} set $s=\head{v}$. \While{$v$ is not in $s$ and $s$ is full}{ set $s$ as $s^+$. } \If{$v$ is not in $s$ and $s$ is not full}{ return “$v$ is not in the system”. } \While{$s$ is not $\head{v}$}{ name $u$ as the least recently accessed item in $s^-$. $swap(v,u)$. set $s$ as $s^-$. }
elements.sent-2411-126: Accessing only involves items that are already in the system.
elements.sent-2411-127: If an item is not already in the system, it is handled by insertion procedure, discussed next.
elements.sent-2411-128: We now focus on how the insertion and deletion of items (or servers) are performed.
elements.sent-2411-129: Now that both numbers of items and servers could change, we define $m^t$ to be the number of items and $n^t$ to be the number of servers at time~$t$.
elements.sent-2411-130: Given the dynamicity of $n^t$ and $m^t$, changing the servers' capacity is essential to ensure our capacity constraint on each server.. Capacity Change.
elements.sent-2411-131: We perform capacity changes in phases.
elements.sent-2411-132: A phase ends in one of two scenarios:.
elements.sent-2411-133: We keep this difference in a parameter called $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-134: At the end of a phase, we need to decrease or increase the capacity of all servers to adhere to the capacity constraint based on the number of items and servers.
elements.sent-2411-135: In case of a capacity decrease, we might have some servers that have more items than their capacity.
elements.sent-2411-136: With the capacity increase, servers that were previously full are not full anymore, affecting access operations.
elements.sent-2411-137: To remedy this issue, we first define valid items for a server $s$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Filling unused capacity: In this case, we fill the additional slot that has been created in $s$ by moving the newest valid item from the back of $s$ (server $s^+$ and beyond) to $s$.
elements.sent-2411-139: In particular, we first search the server $s^+$ for a valid item and move it to $s$.
elements.sent-2411-140: If there is none, we look into the next servers until reaching a non-full server (given that we have extra capacity for all servers, as we have extra capacity per server, i.e, $\alpha \ge 1$, such a non-full server could always be found).
elements.sent-2411-141: In case no such valid item exists until the next non-full server, it means no items from the server in front of $s$ have been moved over $s$, so we keep $s$ as non-full and stop , while being sure that Invariant~\ref{inv: between is intact.}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{\system Item Insertion/Deletion} \label{alg: item} use Algorithm~\ref{Alg: MTHC} to search for the item $v$. \If{the request is item insertion and $v$ was not found}{ Put $v$ in the first non-full server after $\head{v}$. increase $\delta$ and run change capacities if $\delta \ge n$. } \If{the request is item deletion and $v$ was found}{ remove $v$ from its current server. fill the extra capacity. decrease $\delta$ and change capacities if $\delta \le -n$. }
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{\system Capacity Change} \label{alg: cap} Set the capacity of all servers equal to $\capUp{t}$. Set $\delta$ equal to $0$. \For{all servers $s$ starting from server $0$}{ \If{$s$ was full in the previous phase and had extra capacity}{ \While{Server $s$ has extra capacity}{ set $s' = s^+$. \While{$s'$ does not have a valid item}{ $s' = s'+1$ } bring the newest valid item from $s'$ to $s$, } } \If{$s$ has more items than its capacity}{ move the least recently accessed item of $s$ to $s^+$. } }
elements.sent-2411-142: Moving out extra load: In this case, we need to move out the extra item $v$.
elements.sent-2411-143: For that, we move the least recently accessed item of server $s$ to server $s^+$.
elements.sent-2411-144: Combining the above procedures, we end up with the algorithm to keep the capacity of servers consistent, Algorithm~\ref{alg: cap .}
elements.sent-2411-145: Now, we discuss how insertion and deletions are designed on top of capacity change operations.. Inserting an item.
elements.sent-2411-146: To insert $v$, after searching for it, we assume that we have not found it, otherwise, we do not need to do anything, to avoid duplicates of the same item.
elements.sent-2411-147: Then, we place $v$ in $s^{nf}$, the first non-full server after $\head{v}$.
elements.sent-2411-148: As $s^{nf}$ is non-full, we can place $v$ in it, without moving other items.. Deleting an item.
elements.sent-2411-149: To delete $v$, if we could not find it between $\head{v}$ and the next non-full server, it is nowhere else to be found (based on Invariant~\ref{inv: between ), and nothing else is needed to be done.}
elements.sent-2411-150: If the item was found, we would remove it from the server that currently contains $v$, $host(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-151: After that, our algorithm needs to fill the extra capacity created.
elements.sent-2411-152: If we can not find a valid item to fill the extra capacity, we do not take any further action.. Inserting a server.
elements.sent-2411-153: We first determine the position of the server using our hash function.
elements.sent-2411-154: We then tag the new server as a server that was previously full and run Algorithm~\ref{alg: cap to bring valid items back to this server.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Keep in mind that there might not be enough valid items, and this server can remain non-full.. Deleting a server.
elements.sent-2411-156: To delete server $s$, we move all of its items to the server $s^+$ temporally, and then remove it from the system.
elements.sent-2411-157: After running Algorithm~\ref{alg: cap to adjust the capacity of all servers, we move the extra items that might be accumulated on top of server $s^+$ to the next servers, giving priority to the oldest items in each server to stay in that server.}
elements.sent-2411-158: An important invariant of discussed insertion and deletion operations is that they keep the relative order of items.

elements.sent-2411-159: In this section, we provide the competitiveness analysis of our algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-160: We first start by stating a general method, and then discuss the competitiveness of access and insertion/deletion operations separately.. Decomposition to \ServerLists.
elements.sent-2411-161: For analytical purposes, we decompose the ring used by \system into a set of full servers, except their last server: a set of \ServerLists.
elements.sent-2411-162: Informally, a \ServerList is a consecutive subsequence of servers on the ring (a formal and more general definition follows, see Figure~\ref{fig: decomposition for an example).}
elements.sent-2411-163: Such a decomposition is possible because of additional extra capacity, which results in non-full servers.
elements.sent-2411-164: Those non-full servers are at the end of a \ServerList, and the server after them is the start of another \ServerList.
elements.sent-2411-165: Our algorithm is designed such that no item moves over the non-full servers; hence the interactions inside a \ServerList do not affect other \ServerLists.
elements.sent-2411-166: The traditional list access problem~\cite{SleatorT85 considers a list of servers (with capacity one) on a line, and all items share the same head.}
elements.sent-2411-167: A \ServerList is an extension of a list in two directions:.
elements.sent-2411-168: We allow the list to have multiple heads, i.e., items can be assigned to one of the servers from a subset of $0 < b \le n$ servers, which we call heads of the \ServerList.
elements.sent-2411-169: We denote these heads by $\{head_1,\dots,head_b\}$\footnote{This definition generalizes definition needed to prove the competitiveness of \system, in which every server would be a head.. .}
elements.sent-2411-170: We allow servers to have capacities larger than one and equal to $c$, and we consider all of the servers to be full except the last one.
elements.sent-2411-171: In the following, we will show that a competitive result can be achieved for \ServerLists.
elements.sent-2411-172: The algorithm that we use for access in a \ServerList is the same as Algorithm~\ref{Alg: MTHC .}
elements.sent-2411-173: We can see the steps of the access algorithm on a \ServerList in Figure~\ref{fig: MTHC .}
elements.sent-2411-174: In the \ServerList maintained by Algorithm~\ref{Alg: MTHC , between two items with the same head, the item that has been accessed more recently is closer to the head.}
elements.sent-2411-175: It is because after an access request, the least recently accessed item is moved to its head, and we have chosen the oldest items from servers in between to move back.

elements.sent-2411-271: This theorem shows, that in expectation, how we can conclude that the amortized cost of each operation is constant, given that access operations have a constant cost, and insertion and deletions are rare enough to match our assumption.
elements.sent-2411-272: We start this proof by looking back at the competitiveness of access operations given item (or server) insertion (or deletion), and then focus on the competitiveness of those operations themselves.

elements.sent-2411-276: We have concluded in Theorem~\ref{thm: MTHC that an access operation inside a \ServerList is constant competitive.}
elements.sent-2411-277: We now detail why that remains true considering items (or server) insertion (or deletion).
elements.sent-2411-278: Given Invariant~\ref{inv: order from the previous section, the relative order of items that were previously in the system remains the same after insertions or deletions; in other words, the least recently used items are closer to their own head in each \ServerList.}
elements.sent-2411-279: Furthermore, the newly added item itself is always added at the end of a \ServerList; hence it does not affect the order of items in that or any given \ServerLists.
elements.sent-2411-280: After an insertion or deletion, there is a possibility that a \ServerList splits (or a newly added server becomes its own server), or two \ServerLists might join together:.
elements.sent-2411-281: In case of a \ServerList splitting, there would be no item left of the first \ServerList in the other \ServerList.
elements.sent-2411-282: Hence, there would not be any shared inversions between the two split \ServerLists, i.e., there would not be an inversion that consists of an item in one \ServerList and another item in the other.
elements.sent-2411-283: Hence, our potential function which is based on inversions can be computed considering inversions of two \ServerLists separately.
elements.sent-2411-284: In case of two \ServerLists merging, as two \ServerLists lists did not have a shared item before, there were no shared inversions.
elements.sent-2411-285: Therefore, the potential function after the merge does not differ from when two \ServerLists were split, i.e., the sum of potential functions of previously split \ServerLists.
elements.sent-2411-286: Therefore, in both cases, the arguments in Theorem~\ref{thm: MTHC still hold and the constant competitiveness of \system holds.}
elements.sent-2411-288: We discussed in Lemma~\ref{lem: cap why capacity changes are competitive.}

elements.sent-2411-291: Server deletion and insertion do not introduce an extra cost themselves, but rather the movement of items is the costly part.

elements.sent-2411-304: Our synthetic data set ranges over with various temporal localities.
elements.sent-2411-305: By varying temporal locality, we can adjust the number of consecutive repetitions of items that are assigned to a server.
elements.sent-2411-306: Such temporal bursts are a typical pattern in communication traffic To formalize temporal locality, we consider the probability of an item being accessed consecutively.
elements.sent-2411-307: The temporal locality parameter has range of $[0.1,0.9]$, and has the value $0.7$ by default to simulate bursts in the input.
elements.sent-2411-308: We use $10,000$ and $1,000,000$ by default for the number of items and servers, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-310: Given that we aim to utilize most of the servers' storage, we consider the hardware failure and replacement of servers to be independent of each other.
elements.sent-2411-311: As a result, in our evaluations, we used a Poisson distribution to simulate server (i.e., hard disk) insertion and deletion processes~\cite{YeXT13 .}
elements.sent-2411-313: The best choice of a hash function for the consistent hashing method has been a long long-held debate~\cite{AamandT19 .}
elements.sent-2411-314: For our plots, we used more practically safe and secure SHA-512~\cite{DobraunigEM16Sha .}
elements.sent-2411-315: However, our implementation also supports 5-independent hash functions~\cite{K-independent1,5Independent1 .. Extra capacity.}
elements.sent-2411-316: Our algorithm, \system, and \WBL's algorithm rely on having extra capacity per each server.
elements.sent-2411-317: In our simulations, the default $\alpha$ value for our algorithm is $4$, i.e., four extra slots added to the minimum capacity of each server ($\lceil \frac{m {n \rceil$).}}
elements.sent-2411-318: In our evaluations, we considered the constant factor of $1.25$ for \WBL, based on their recommendation~\cite{MirrokniTZ18 .}
elements.sent-2411-320: To bootstrap an instance of consistent hashing, we need to set up a number of servers initially.
elements.sent-2411-321: We consider this value to be equal to $20$ by default.. Stale time.
elements.sent-2411-322: With stale time, we aim to simulate the time-out option for each item stored in an instance of consistent hashing.
elements.sent-2411-323: To do so, we delete an item after a fixed period of time, which has been set to $20$ minutes by default in our simulations.

elements.sent-2411-325: We compare our three main algorithms in terms of storage utilization and amortized access cost per request, given the real-world dataset.
elements.sent-2411-326: Regarding storage utilization, using \WBL algorithm with a multiplication factor of $1.25$, the storage utilization only increases by $40$ percent from $50$ of the traditional algorithm (cf.~Figure~\ref{fig: intro-prac ).}
elements.sent-2411-327: However, as also shown in the~figure, this better storage utilization comes with an access cost of $12$ per item on average.
elements.sent-2411-328: Using \system, we can achieve closer to 100\% storage utilization, 90\% to be exact.
elements.sent-2411-329: Furthermore, we require more than 61\% less access cost compared to \WBL.
elements.sent-2411-331: When we increase temporal locality (i.e., the probability of an item being requested consecutively), we can see a decrease in the cost of \system, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig: res-loc-aug .}
elements.sent-2411-332: That is because the item that has been moved to its head is going to be accessed again soon.
elements.sent-2411-333: The increase in the \WBL's access cost is because an item from further away might be accessed more often.
elements.sent-2411-335: In this experiment, we consider that all algorithms have the same capacity on the servers.
elements.sent-2411-336: As expected, \system performs much better when the additional capacity is small, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig: sameCap2 .}
elements.sent-2411-337: Furthermore, for such a fixed capacity we can see our algorithm can tolerate more items with a lower cost, see Figure~\ref{fig: sameCap1 .}
elements.sent-2411-338: Effect of changing the number of servers.
elements.sent-2411-339: By increasing the number of servers (see Figure~\ref{fig: res-intial ), our algorithm and WBL both observe a reduced access cost.}
elements.sent-2411-340: We can report from our observation that the sharp drop in access cost is because as we increase the number of servers, there is a higher chance that more non-full servers appear between a series of full servers, i.e., the maximum length of consecutive full servers decreases.
elements.sent-2411-341: This result is consistent with the outcome of Lemma~\ref{Lemma: expected consecutive full that shows that the number of consecutive full servers decreases as the number of servers grows.}

elements.sent-2411-343: We consider the effects of two further benchmarking parameters: the number of servers and stale time.
elements.sent-2411-344: By increasing the number of servers (see Figure~\ref{fig: res-intial ), both algorithms observe a reduced access cost.}
elements.sent-2411-345: Considering the stale time (Figure~\ref{fig: stale ), we saw an increase in the maximum capacity needed for servers, which was predictable, as more items will be in the system with a higher stale time.}
elements.sent-2411-346: However, the increase in the access cost of \WBL means that it performs worse in terms of both access cost and memory utilization for an increased stale time.
elements.sent-2411-347: Our work builds upon literature in the context of consistent hashing and self-adjusting data structures.
elements.sent-2411-348: We review them in turn, showing how previous consistent hashing ideas result in increasing storage utilization, and how self-adjustments can lead to decreasing access time.
elements.sent-2411-350: Consistent hashing was first introduced by Karger et al.~\cite{KargerClassic1 , building on top of the classical problem of ``balls into bins"~\cite{azar1994power .}}
elements.sent-2411-351: The concept quickly became very important for distributed and peer-to-peer systems~\cite{DistrbutedBook20,LampingV14 .}
elements.sent-2411-352: The classic use case of consistent hashing is in the design of distributed hash tables in peer-to-peer systems like Chord~\cite{Chord03 or Kademlia~\cite{Kademlia02 and more~\cite{NaorW03,LuaCPSL05 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-353: The second use case of consistent hashing is in web caching~\cite{WebCaching99, RevisitCH21 in cloud systems (our experimental results explore this second scenario).}
elements.sent-2411-354: Recently, a team at Google implemented consistent hashing with bounded loads, showing the effects of bounding load in their content hosting system~\cite{MirrokniTZ18 .}
elements.sent-2411-355: Inspired by the work of Google, the team behind Viemo load balancer implemented a similar algorithm as part of HAProxy~\cite{tarreau2017haproxy that resulted in 8-fold improvement in their cache bandwidth~\cite{rodland2016Vimeo .}}
elements.sent-2411-356: Traditionally, consistent hashing approaches abstract the routing protocol, i.e., allowing for flexible routing, as we considered in our paper.
elements.sent-2411-357: Some of the related works suggest curtain approaches for routing.
elements.sent-2411-358: One of the proposals is using virtual servers~\cite{Virtual01, Virtual04, SPAA04, AamandKT21, VirtualNodes2020 , in which a server is not responsible only for one hash value; but two or more.}
elements.sent-2411-359: On top of complicated routing, this method requires more storage to store the relation of each server with each hash function.
elements.sent-2411-360: Another idea is to change the assignment of the next server for an item based on the number of full servers it observed so far~\cite{RevisitCH21 .}
elements.sent-2411-361: This approach requires dedicated routing tables, and can only support insertion and deletion in specific scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-362: Furthermore, we point out two differences of our model with traditional caching models~\cite{CachingMarking,Roughgarden19 .}
elements.sent-2411-363: Firstly, in such models cache hits come with zero cost, while in our model, accessing the first server has a cost of one.
elements.sent-2411-364: Secondly, in our model, all servers provide the same speed in terms of accessing items, however, in the caching hierarchies, usually we see a slowdown when we get further away from processing units.
elements.sent-2411-365: Self-adjusting data structures and decreasing access time.
elements.sent-2411-366: Self-adjusting data structures have been explored for almost half a century now~\cite{CoffmanD73 .}
elements.sent-2411-367: The pioneering work of Sleator and Tarjan~\cite{SleatorT85 used the amortized analysis for the online algorithm of list update.}
elements.sent-2411-368: To the best of our knowledge (check~\cite{INFOCOM23 for an updated list of papers), there are no results on the list update problem with capacity or multiple heads.}
elements.sent-2411-369: The notion of a self-adjusting hash table was first suggested by Pagli in 1985~\cite{Pagli85SelfAdjustingHash and then by Wogulis~\cite{Wogulis89 , both as heuristics.}}
elements.sent-2411-370: Self-adjusting hashing gained attention recently~\cite{LRUHashing18 , especially when Microsoft's team proposed VIP Hashing~\cite{VIPHashing .}}
elements.sent-2411-371: However, there are significant differences between our work, and the other suggested self-adjusting hash tables.
elements.sent-2411-372: First of all, we emphasize that comparing the performance of our algorithm with methods of~\cite{Pagli85SelfAdjustingHash,Wogulis89, LRUHashing18, VIPHashing is not possible: they aim to optimize intra-server costs, but we aim to minimize the inter-server costs.}
elements.sent-2411-373: On top of that, our work provides additional benefits such as: (1) proving competitive guarantees formally, (2) balancing between storage utilization and access cost, and (3) analyzing the effects of temporal locality.
elements.sent-2411-374: Furthermore, compared to VIP Hashing, our algorithm does not require a complicated learning phase and can be implemented with the change of a few lines in existing systems.
elements.sent-2411-375: Motivated by the dynamic temporal structure of demands in networked and distributed systems, we have introduced an adaptive approach to improve the performance of distributed hash tables.
elements.sent-2411-376: Our main contribution is a constant-competitive algorithm for self-adjustments guaranteeing bounded loads.
elements.sent-2411-377: In our future research, we aim to improve our approximation bounds and explore opportunities to render other distributed datastructures self-adjusting.
elements.sent-2411-378: From a practical point of view, we are working on incorporating our algorithms into real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-379: In particular, we are working on an updated code for HAProxy, and also to improve load balancing for virtual IP address assignment (e.g., as proposed by Google~\cite{beyer2016site ).. \balance \bibliographystyle{plainurl .}}
